field-notes:
Change Management:
Issues often arise during company mergers (Topic 1.1.3). Key problems: Language differences (international mergers), software incompatibility, and workforce retraining.

Installation Methods (Topic 1.1.5):
Parallel Running: Old and new systems run together. Pro: Safe (backup available). Con: Expensive (duplicate workload).
Direct Changeover: Instant switch. Pro: Fast, cheapest. Con: High risk (no fallback if new system fails).

Usability vs Accessibility (Topic 1.2.12):
Usability: Ease of use/Efficiency. Issues: Small screens, battery life.
Accessibility: Access for those with disabilities. Solutions: Text-to-speech, Braille keyboards, Voice recognition.
field-keywords: Change Management, Legacy System, SaaS, Failover, Data Migration, Alpha/Beta Testing, User Documentation

checklist-data.text: 1.1.1 Identify context for which a new system is planned.
⭐ 1.1.3 Compatibility issues (legacy systems/mergers).
⭐ 1.1.5 Evaluate alternative installation processes (Parallel, Pilot, etc).
1.1.7 Suggest various types of testing (Alpha, Beta, Debugging).
⭐ 1.1.11 Identify a range of causes of data loss.
1.2.1 Define hardware, software, peripheral, network, HR.
1.2.7 Construct representations of requirements (Flowcharts, DFDs).
⭐ 1.2.12 Define the term usability & accessibility.

field-notes:
CPU Registers (Topic 2.1.1):
MAR (Memory Address Register): Holds the address of the data/instruction to be fetched.
MDR (Memory Data Register): Holds the actual data fetched from memory.

Cache Memory (Topic 2.1.3):
Small, high-speed memory inside CPU. Holds frequently used data to prevent the CPU waiting for the slower RAM. Speed mismatch solution.

RAM vs ROM (Topic 2.1.2):
RAM: Volatile, R/W, stores running OS/Programs.
ROM: Non-volatile, Read-only, stores BIOS/Bootloader.
field-keywords: CPU, ALU, CU, Registers, MAR, MDR, Cache, RAM, ROM, Persistent Storage, OS, Binary, Logic Gates

checklist-data.text: ⭐ 2.1.1 Outline architecture of CPU (ALU, CU, Registers).
⭐ 2.1.3 Explain the use of cache memory.
2.1.4 Explain the machine instruction cycle.
2.1.6 Describe main functions of an Operating System.
⭐ 2.1.9 Define bit, byte, binary, denary, hexadecimal.
2.1.11 Define Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR).
2.1.13 Construct logic diagrams.

field-notes:
VPN (Virtual Private Network) (Topic 3.1.5):
Allows secure work from home (telecommuting).
Keywords: Tunneling (hides data), Encryption (scrambles data), Gateway (entry point).

Packet Switching (Topic 3.1.11):
1. Data broken into packets.
2. Packets travel independently via different routes.
3. Reassembled at destination using sequence numbers.
Benefit: Robust (if one line fails, packet reroutes).
field-keywords: LAN, WAN, VLAN, VPN, OSI Model, Protocol, Packet Switching, Compression, Wireless, Encryption

checklist-data.text: 3.1.1 Identify different types of networks (LAN, WAN, VPN, PAN).
3.1.3 Describe layers of communication (OSI model awareness).
⭐ 3.1.5 Evaluate the use of a VPN.
⭐ 3.1.6 Define protocol and data packet.
⭐ 3.1.11 Explain packet switching.
3.1.14 Describe characteristics of wireless networks.
3.1.15 Describe network security (Encryption, MAC address).

field-notes:
Recursion (Topic 5.1.1):
A function calling itself. Good for tree traversals (In-order/Pre-order).
Risk: Stack Overflow if too many calls are made (memory fills up).

Linked List vs Array (Topic 5.1.11):
Array: Static size, faster access (index), contiguous memory.
Linked List: Dynamic size, slower access (must traverse), uses extra memory for pointers.

Trees (Topic 5.1.17):
Child: Node with a predecessor. Leaf: Node with no children.
field-keywords: Recursion, 2D Arrays, Stack, Queue, Linked List, Binary Tree, LIFO, FIFO, Dynamic Data Structure

checklist-data.text: ⭐ 5.1.1 Identify situations requiring recursive thinking.
5.1.4 Describe characteristics of 2D arrays.
⭐ 5.1.6 Describe characteristics of a Stack (LIFO).
5.1.8 Describe characteristics of a Queue (FIFO).
⭐ 5.1.11 Describe features of dynamic data structures.
5.1.12 Describe how linked lists operate logically.
⭐ 5.1.17 Sketch binary trees.

field-notes:
Polling vs Interrupts (Topic 6.1.7):
Polling: CPU wastes time actively checking if a device needs attention.
Interrupt: Device sends a signal to CPU only when needed. CPU stops current task to handle it. Much more efficient.

Paging (Topic 6.1.7):
Mechanism for Virtual Memory. Fixed-size blocks of data (pages) are swapped between RAM and Hard Disk to allow programs larger than physical RAM to run.
field-keywords: System Resources, Scheduling, Paging, Interrupt, Polling, Virtual Memory, Dedicated OS

checklist-data.text: 6.1.1 Identify resources to be managed (RAM, Processor, Bandwidth).
6.1.3 Identify limitations of resources.
⭐ 6.1.5 Explain OS role in resource management.
⭐ 6.1.7 Outline scheduling, polling, interrupts, paging.
6.1.9 Outline how OS hides hardware complexity.

field-notes:
The Control Loop (Topic 7.1.4):
1. Sensor: Reads analogue environment data.
2. ADC: Converts to Digital.
3. Processor: Compares reading to pre-set value.
4. Transducer/Actuator: Performs action (e.g., turns on heater).

Feedback (Topic 7.1.5):
Essential for a Closed Loop system. The output impacts the next input (e.g., Cruise Control maintains speed by monitoring current speed).
field-keywords: Centralized, Distributed, Microprocessor, Sensor, Transducer, Feedback, Embedded System, Autonomous Agent

checklist-data.text: 7.1.1 Discuss a range of control systems (e.g. GPS, Traffic lights).
⭐ 7.1.2 Outline uses of microprocessors and sensors.
⭐ 7.1.4 Explain relationship: Sensor -> Processor -> Transducer.
⭐ 7.1.5 Describe the role of feedback (Closed Loop).
7.1.7 Compare centralized vs distributed control systems.
7.1.8 Outline role of autonomous agents.
